Gaming_Tech.title: Teen hackers jailed after live streaming cyber attack on TfL
Gaming_Tech.text:
Two men who carried out a cyber-attack which crippled Transport For London (TfL) when they were teenagers have both been sentenced to five years and six months in prison.

Owen Flowers, 18, from Walsall, and Thalha Jubair, 20, from east London, pleaded guilty in June to carrying out the hack in 2024.

They were described as computer-obsessed loners who carried out the hack as part of the cyber crime collective known as Scattered Spider.

The cyber-attack disrupted TfL's online services for months, stole the personal data of millions of people and left all 27,000 TfL employees needing to reset their passwords in person.

Woolwich Crown Court heard the criminals streamed their 16 hour long cyber-attack online.

The National Crime Agency (NCA) said the rise of young hackers in the UK as one of the biggest threats to the nation's cyber security.

Flowers was 17 and Jubair was 18 when they hacked into the capital's transport authority at 1700 on 31 August.

Telegram messages sent between the pair showed them boasting about gaining access to TfL's database of people with Oyster cards.

The teens then searched the list for the personal details of London celebrities, before attempting to access banking details.

"Scattered Spider is creating webs on the London Underground," Flowers would later joke - referring to the loosely coordinated group of young English-speaking hackers.

The group has been linked to dozens of other cyber-attacks including on retailers Marks and Spencer and the Co-op.

In the last two years young men and boys have been arrested for Scattered Spider hacks in the UK, US, Spain and Finland.

The TfL hack saw the data of millions of customers stolen in a spree which started on a Saturday night to maximise their chances of not being discovered by staff.

As revealed by the BBC, the database is still being shared in criminal groups and contains the details of as many as 10 million TfL customers.

Jubair and Flowers, gained access to the data by tricking a phone help desk worker.

They convinced the person to reset the password of an employee they were impersonating.

TfL was alerted to the breach by the NCA and worked to kick the hackers out - but not before the criminals gained the details of millions of people.

The transport authority said the hack could have caused widespread disruption had its IT team not stopped the hackers by logging out all staff - and eventually disconnecting TfL systems from the internet.

In total, 148 technology systems became inoperable and heavily disrupted services including Dial-a-ride - used by disabled and vulnerable Londoners.

TfL says the financial impact of the hack was £29m - in addition it estimates that the incident cost £10m in lost income.

Woolwich Crown Court heard both men were loners who had few offline friends, and spent most of their time online unsupervised.

Judge Mr Justice Turner cited their young age and autism diagnoses as mitigating factors in his sentencing remarks.

Police say Flowers rarely left his house and spent most of his time in his bedroom using his computer.

As previously revealed by the BBC, he had first been given a cease and desist order for minor cyber crime in October 2023 shortly after he turned 16.

A few months later the teenager, who was living with his grandmother and uncle, committed a series of cyber offences.

Flowers was eventually arrested in September 2024 in connection with the TfL attack.

Video of his arrest shows the teenager laughing as he is taken into custody.

In the arrest raid, investigators caught Flowers in the act of hacking two US healthcare providers.

Messages he sent showed him joking about the hacks potentially "killing a 90-year-old on life support".

He pleaded guilty to offences relating to those hacks, along with the TfL cyber-attack.

Police also seized cryptocurrency holdings worth around £1m.

Although Flowers and Jubair were said to have accumulated millions of pounds in stolen or ransomed cryptocurrency, police say their motives were likely about online notoriety more than financial gain.

Like Flowers, Jubair had been known to police for years.

Woolwich Crown Court heard that whilst in prison awaiting trial, both Jubair and Flowers were discovered with contraband phones.

Recovered messages show the men continuing to discuss and coordinate future cyber-attacks.

After the sentencing NCA deputy director Paul Foster, head of its National Cyber Crime Unit, said the case highlighted the challenges posed by home grown hackers.

"The online world can expose young people to harmful influences and criminal communities far beyond their front door," he said.

"Parents, carers, educators, technology companies and law enforcement, the whole of society, we all have a role to play in helping to keep young people safe online.

He claimed Scattered Spider was "heavily degraded and disrupted" as a result of the arrests, but cyber security analyst Allison Nixon said it would do little to put off young boys from cyber crime.

"Policymakers need to address this as a violent youth gang problem, with a gang culture that idolizes the destruction of society and maximising victim harm," she said.
Gaming_Tech.description: Two men who carried out a cyber-attack which crippled Transport For London (TfL) when they were teenagers have both been sentenced to five years and six months in prison. Owen Flowers, 18, from Walsall, and Thalha Jubair, 20, from east London, pleaded guilty in June to carrying out the hack in 2024. They were...
Gaming_Tech.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: The Odyssey film fans inspired to go back to the original Homer poem
Entertainment.text:
Sir Christopher Nolan's big-screen version of The Odyssey has become one of the box office hits of the year - and has also sparked interest in the original Greek myth, with book sales and audiobook listening figures rising sharply.

The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices globally in its opening weekend, including $52m (£39m) on giant Imax screens.

Some of those fans have been inspired to go back to the source, with sales of print editions rising by 700% in the UK last week compared with the same period in 2025.

An audio version read by Sir Stephen Fry is now in the Spotify UK audiobook top 10, while Sir Ian McKellen's narration has entered the equivalent chart in the US.

Almost 8,000 print copies of The Odyssey were sold in the UK last week, taking the total so far this year to 47,000. That's a 95% year-on-year increase, according to NielsenIQ BookData.

British chain Waterstones said there had been "phenomenal" demand, particularly for the 2017 translation by Emily Wilson, which Sir Christopher has cited as one of his sources.

"We got more stock in but we weren't anticipating quite the uplift, particularly on Emily Wilson," Waterstones chief operating officer Kate Skipper told BBC Radio 4's Today programme.

"So we're getting deliveries every day and they're flying out as soon as they land in the shops."

Wilson herself welcomed the impact of the film, but pointed out that Sir Christopher has left out some crucial elements.

"I want to celebrate the fact that so many more people are aware of The Odyssey and were brought back to the poem," she told the Associated Press, external.

"But at the same time, I had the sense that Christopher Nolan was trying to pack everything in while missing themes that were essential to the poem."

Many of those who have gone back to the ancient story are younger people who are listening to the audiobook version.

Listening figures on Spotify have risen by about 680% since the film's release on Friday, Variety reported, external - with more than 80% of the listeners being Gen Z or millennials.

In cinemas, the film's global takings include £12.6m from the UK - making it the second-biggest opening weekend in the country so far this year, behind Toy Story 5.

In the process, The Odyssey broke the record for the highest takings in an opening weekend for a film on Imax screens in the UK, according to industry publication Screen Daily, external.

The three-hour epic, which stars Matt Damon, Tom Holland and Anne Hathaway, was the first theatrical release to be shot entirely with Imax 70mm film cameras.

While there are more than 50 Imax screens in the UK, only three are showing it using 70mm film projectors - the BFI and Science Museum in London, and Printworks in Manchester.

"You do feel like you're very close to the characters, you're immersed in the action. So it is a heightened experience, I would say," film critic and Girls on Film podcast host Anna Smith told the Today programme.

Imax screenings are more expensive than regular cinema tickets, however.

"If you have the money and you've been lucky enough to book, I don't think you would regret seeing it in Imax 70mm," Metro film critic Tori Brazier said.

"But at the same time it's very expensive and we do live in a world where lots of people watch films on laptop screens and phone screens now, so if you're going to the cinema you're going to have a big-screen experience regardless."
Entertainment.description: Sir Christopher Nolan's big-screen version of The Odyssey has become one of the box office hits of the year - and has also sparked interest in the original Greek myth, with book sales and audiobook listening figures rising sharply. The director's take on Homer's epic poem took $264m (£198m) at cinema box offices...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Oasis overtake The Beatles in all-time UK album chart
Entertainment.text:
Oasis's second album (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK's most popular albums of all-time, external.

The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on the all-time best-sellers list, which has been published by the Official Charts Company.

The Beatles' 1967 psychedelic masterpiece Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band, which was third on the same list a decade ago, external, has now dropped to number five.

Queen's 1981 Greatest Hits collection remains the biggest seller overall with 1992's Abba Gold in second place, while Adele's 2011 album 21 has moved up to fourth place.

The all-time list was released on Wednesday to mark the 70th anniversary of the weekly UK album chart.

In total, (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has racked up 6.2 million UK chart units - streams and sales combined - according to the Official Charts Company.

Last year, there was renewed interest in Oasis's music due to their much publicised worldwide reunion tour. The setlist lent heavily on tracks from the album, including Some Might Say and Champagne Supernova.

In 2022, Queen's compilation LP made history as the first album to pass seven million UK chart units.

Only Swedish pop maestros Abba have breached the seven million mark since, making it an elite club.

Meanwhile, Fleetwood Mac's 1977 classic Rumours and Bob Marley's best-of album Legend have both moved into the all-time top 10 over the past 10 years.

UK Official Albums Chart - all-time top 10

Greatest Hits - Queen

(What's the Story) Morning Glory - Oasis

Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band - The Beatles

Rumours - Fleetwood Mac

The Dark Side of the Moon - Pink Floyd

Thriller - Michael Jackson

Legend - Bob Marley and The Wailers

Brothers in Arms - Dire Straits

(What's the Story) Morning Glory? received surprisingly mixed reviews from the British music press when it was released in October 1995. But it went on to win best British album at the 1996 Brit Awards and has stood the test of time.

Wonderwall, from the album, became hugely popular again this summer with England fans and players singing it together at the World Cup in North America.

The booming rock ballad Don't Look Back in Anger also took on a greater significance after it was sung, impromptu, at a vigil for those who lost their lives in the 2017 Manchester Arena attack.

Becca Monahan and Chris Austin, interim co-managing directors of the Official Charts, congratulated Oasis on the album's achievements in a joint statement.

"Three decades on, its scale and influence remain extraordinary - a defining British record that continues to unite generations, fill stadiums and soundtrack lives," they noted.

Oasis Songwriter Noel Gallagher was born three days after The Beatles released their Sgt Pepper album.
Entertainment.description: Oasis's second album (What's The Story) Morning Glory? has officially overtaken the biggest-selling album by their heroes The Beatles in the list of the UK's most popular albums of all-time, external. The Gallagher brothers' 1995 album, which contains hits like Wonderwall and Don't Look Back in Anger, is third on...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Kaylee Hottle: 'Devastated' Millie Bobby Brown leads tributes
Entertainment.text:
Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18.

Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning.

The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021 blockbuster Godzilla vs Kong.

Bobby Brown shared her sadness, alongside a black and white image of Hottle, to her Instagram story.

Over the photo, the Stranger Things star wrote: "I'm so devastated to hear this.

"You will be deeply missed Kaylee."

Hottle played a young orphan named Jia in the monster film series, and reprised the role in the 2024 sequel Godzilla x Kong: The New Empire.

British actress Rebecca Hall, who featured in both films with Hottle, paid tribute by sharing pictures of them together on set.

"My heart goes out to your family. You will be missed Kaylee," Hall said.

Fellow co-star and Mexican actress Eiza González said her "heart is absolutely broken" as she posted a photo of them posing in costume.

"I'm devastated. I adore you," wrote González.

"I know heaven has the sweetest kindest girl. We will miss you terribly."

Legendary Pictures, the production company behind the Godzilla movies, described Hottle as a "cherished member" of the production.

"Through her unforgettable portrayal of Jia, Kaylee brought extraordinary heart, humanity, and light to Godzilla, Kong, and the Monsterverse," the statement on Instagram said., external

"Her kindness, warmth, and remarkable talent touched everyone fortunate enough to work alongside her."

Oscar winning deaf actress Marlee Matlin also shared a photo to her Instagram, external of herself and fellow actress Shoshannah Stern alongside Hottle.

Matlin said she was "absolutely gutted" about the passing of "sweet Kaylee Hottle."

"May her beauty and talent be a memory for eternity."

Over the years, Hottle had worked to advocate for the deaf community.

Teri Devine, associate director of inclusion at UK charity Royal National Institute for Deaf People (RNID), told BBC Newsbeat that Hottle helped "pave the way" for greater representation.

She described her as a "passionate advocate for the deaf community and deaf actors in Hollywood" and said she used her platform to "raise awareness and inspire others to drive positive change for people who are deaf or have hearing loss".

"Representation of people who are deaf or have hearing loss in the spotlight is incredibly important," Devine added.

"Kaylee helped to improve understanding, challenge misconceptions, and prove there are no limits to what people who are deaf or have hearing loss can achieve."

The actress also set an example to young people, a spokesperson for the National Deaf Children's Society said.

Martin McLean, the charity's deputy director of policy and campaigns, said Hottle "inspired deaf children and young people by showing that there are no limits to what they can achieve".

"Positive deaf role models help deaf young people feel less alone, build confidence in their identity and ambitions, and show them that anything is possible," he added.
Entertainment.description: Millie Bobby Brown is among the stars who have remembered the "talent" and "kindness" of US actress Kaylee Hottle, who died in a car crash aged 18. Hottle was killed in a single-vehicle accident in Frederick, Maryland on Tuesday morning. The young actress, who was deaf, appeared alongside Bobby Brown in the 2021...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Gruesome images in D4vd hearing are 'worst nightmare' for Celeste Rivas Hernandez's family
Entertainment.text:
WARNING: Readers may find details in this article distressing.

Gruesome images of a teenage girl's dismembered corpse have been shown in court before her horrified parents on the opening day of a preliminary hearing in the murder case against the singer who performs under the stage name D4vd.

Prosecutors presented graphic crime scene photos showing 14-year-old Celeste Rivas Hernandez's body when it was found last year inside a Tesla registered to singer David Anthony Burke in Hollywood, Los Angeles.

A homicide detective testified that Burke, 21, had ordered a body bag, chainsaws, an inflatable pool and a shovel online in the days after Celeste's death.

Burke has pleaded not guilty to charges of murdering and sexually abusing the girl.

The grisly details had previously only been described in court filings. Tuesday's hearing marked the first time that the evidence and photographs were shown publicly in court.

Although this is not the trial itself, prosecutors are laying out the evidence they say links Burke to Celeste's death as they seek to convince the judge there is sufficient evidence for the case to proceed to trial.

Prosecutors say Burke stabbed Celeste before dismembering her body with a chainsaw to conceal the killing after years of alleged sexual abuse.

They allege he killed her because she had threatened to reveal his relationship with a minor and destroy his multi-million dollar music career.

For Celeste's parents, it was an ordeal they had prepared themselves for but could never fully anticipate, according to their lawyer.

"This is the first time they've heard this evidence in detail, and they're horrified," the family's attorney, Patrick Steinfeld, told the BBC after the court hearing.

"There was an image of Celeste's torso and her head in the trunk of the car, and the prosecutor had to ask what it was because you could not tell it was a head. It was absolutely deplorable.

"They're at a loss for words and they're just trying to do the best they can."

Steinfeld said Celeste's mother, Mercedes, had been warned in advance that graphic images would be shown.

"I told her she didn't have to be in the courtroom for this part of the hearing," he said.

"But she said, 'I want to be here to show support for Celeste.' She just put her head down and started crying."

He said he had advised both parents that once they saw the images, they would never be able to "unsee" them.

"I encouraged them to step out for a short period of time, and they decided no - they wanted to be here to show they're supporting their daughter."

"They're an extremely private family," he added. "It's a parent's worst nightmare."

The small downtown Los Angeles courtroom was packed long before Tuesday's hearing began.

Journalists, YouTubers, TikTok creators and members of the public had entered a lottery for one of the limited number of seats to watch the proceedings.

There was a palpable sense of anticipation in the room.

The constant chatter stopped when Burke was brought in.

Wearing an orange jail uniform, he was shackled at the wrists and ankles.

His handcuffs were removed, but he remained chained to his chair throughout the hearing.

He briefly smiled at members of his defence team before quietly responding to the judge with "Good morning, Your Honour", and "Yes, Your Honour".

The atmosphere shifted dramatically once Detective Joshua Byers began his testimony.

Before displaying each graphic image, prosecutor Beth Silverman repeatedly turned to Celeste's parents, warning them about what they were about to see and checking whether they wanted to look away.

At one point, Celeste's mother was comforted by supporters seated beside her.

The girl's body was discovered inside the trunk of a Tesla in September 2025 in a Hollywood tow yard.

Detective Byers described opening a cadaver bag inside the car and finding decomposing human remains, including a torso and a head, with another black refuse bag underneath containing severed limbs.

He told the court Celeste's body had decomposed so severely her face was no longer recognisable.

The court also heard evidence showing purchases delivered to Burke's address under an alias after Celeste's death, including chainsaws, body bags and an inflatable pool.

Investigators allege the items were used to dismember and conceal Celeste's body.

The court heard about tattoos prosecutors say connected Burke and Celeste.

The judge was shown photographs of a red-ink tattoo reading "Celeste" on Burke's left ring finger, as well as a "Shhh..." tattoo on his right index finger.

Prosecutors showed a photograph of Celeste with a similar "Shhh..." tattoo on her right index finger and another picture of a hand where the ring and little finger were cut off.

The preliminary hearing is expected to last several days, with prosecutors planning to call additional witnesses as they seek to establish enough evidence for the case to move to a jury trial.
Entertainment.description: WARNING: Readers may find details in this article distressing. Gruesome images of a teenage girl's dismembered corpse have been shown in court before her horrified parents on the opening day of a preliminary hearing in the murder case against the singer who performs under the stage name D4vd. Prosecutors presented...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Rebel Wilson cleared in defamation case brought by actress in her own film
Entertainment.text:
Rebel Wilson has been cleared of accusations that she defamed a young Australian actress, a Sydney court has found.

Charlotte MacInnes, who starred in Wilson's film The Deb, sued the Bridesmaids and Pitch Perfect actress for harming her reputation, over Instagram posts Wilson made in 2024 and 2025.

In the posts, Wilson accused MacInnes of retracting her complaint of sexual harassment against a film producer so she could further her career - a claim MacInnes denied.

In court on Wednesday, Justice Elizabeth Raper dismissed the case, saying the imputation that MacInnes had changed her story about the incident was not defamatory. The judge ordered MacInnes to pay Wilson's costs.

The case centres around an incident involving MacInnes and one of the producers of Wilson's film, Amanda Ghost.

In September 2023, MacInnes and Ghost went for a swim at Bondi Beach where Ghost had an allergic reaction to the cold water, prompting the pair to return to the producer's luxury apartment that she rented.

Ghost had a shower while MacInnes ran a bath for her. The young actress got into the bath to warm up with Ghost joining her shortly after. Both were wearing swimsuits.

Ghost's assistant brought hot drinks for the two women while they were in the bath and sat with them briefly.

The court heard that Wilson later spoke to MacInnes about the incident and while both sides acknowledge the conversation happened, the content of the chat remains in dispute.

Wilson says MacInnes told her the bath incident had made her feel "uncomfortable" but MacInnes denies she said this, and only described the situation as "weird" or "strange and bizarre".

MacInnes had earlier in April told the court that Wilson had "mischaracterised and exploited" an "innocent incident" and her relationship with Wilson deteriorated during filming of The Deb in the months after the bath incident.

Lawyers for MacInnes said Wilson's allegations were "malicious concoctions" and the court heard claims the Hollywood star had ordered a smear website to target Ghost, which Wilson denied.

But Wilson's lawyer argued that MacInnes - who graduated from acting school in Western Australia in 2021 - had not been harmed by the posts, pointing to a recent record deal that she had signed.

In handing down her decision, Justice Raper remarked on the bath incident.

"To say that the circumstance surrounding how they came to be in the bath are unusual would be an understatement," she told the court.

Raper also found that MacInnes had failed to establish the posts had caused or were likely to serious harm.

Wilson posted on Instagram moments after the decision, saying the "process has taught me a lot".

"It has tested me, but it has also reinforced something I've always believed. I want to live my life standing up for what I believe is right, while continuing to create work that brings people joy."

Wilson thanked her supporters and said she was "grateful this chapter has reached its conclusion". She also thanked the judge in her case and "the Australian legal system who I have the utmost respect for".

The BBC has contacted MacInnes for comment.
Entertainment.description: Rebel Wilson has been cleared of accusations that she defamed a young Australian actress, a Sydney court has found. Charlotte MacInnes, who starred in Wilson's film The Deb, sued the Bridesmaids and Pitch Perfect actress for harming her reputation, over Instagram posts Wilson made in 2024 and 2025. In the posts,...
Entertainment.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

Entertainment.title: Judge rules Trump must hand over financial records sought by BBC in defamation case
Entertainment.text:
A judge in Florida has ruled President Donald Trump must hand over financial records sought by the BBC as it defends itself in his $10bn (£7.5bn) defamation lawsuit.

The order came during the first hearing in the case, in which the US president is suing the corporation over an episode of Panorama that edited together different sections of his speech on the day of the US Capitol riot on 6 January 2021.

Judge Enjoliqué Lett granted a motion by the BBC's legal team to compel the release of financial records held by the Donald J Trump Revocable Trust, which he created to manage his businesses while he is president.

The judge's ruling at Tuesday's hearing in Miami can still be appealed.

However, it amounts to a partial success in the BBC's attempt to force Trump to provide evidence for his claim of financial loss due to the Panorama edit.

The judge gave a split decision on a separate request by the BBC for documents relating to the Capitol riot itself.

The defamation case is currently focused on the procedure known as discovery, where the two sides provide internal or other records that could form evidence.

The BBC's lawyers said the corporation had produced 87,000 pages of documents, whereas they said Trump's side had released a "sham production" of only 735 pages, which they said were "news articles" and other material "pulled from the internet".

Counsel for the BBC, Charles Tobin, said this meant the discovery process had been "entirely one-sided".

Counsel for Trump, Alejandro Brito, described the BBC's requests for documents as "oppressive" and "sweeping".

He also opposed the BBC's requests for records about Trump's conversations with aides relating to the Capitol riot, as well as subpoenas issued to federal agencies and former officials.

Trump's legal team accused the BBC of a "fishing expedition" with subpoenas to "harass" former officials.

Much of the argument from Trump's lawyers focused on a letter sent from BBC Chairman Samir Shah to the US president in November last year.

In the letter, Shah apologised for the Panorama clip, which he said unintentionally gave the "mistaken impression that President Trump made a direct call for violent action".

Brito described the letter as a "concession" and "admission" by the BBC.

The BBC has already sought to have the entire case dismissed, arguing the documentary was not broadcast in the US. The court has yet to rule on this.
Entertainment.description: A judge in Florida has ruled President Donald Trump must hand over financial records sought by the BBC as it defends itself in his $10bn (£7.5bn) defamation lawsuit. The order came during the first hearing in the case, in which the US president is suing the corporation over an episode of Panorama that edited...

Entertainment.title: Glasgow School of Art cannot restore fire-hit Mackintosh Building
Entertainment.text:
Glasgow School of Art says it cannot fully restore the city's iconic Mackintosh Building to its former state without additional financial support.

The art school previously insisted it was committed to a faithful reinstatement of the building, which was designed by Charles Rennie Mackintosh but wrecked by two fires in 2014 and 2018.

However it now says "financial clarity", provided by an insurance settlement regarding the 2018 blaze, has shown it "cannot take forward the faithful reinstatement on its own."

Instead it suggests the building has potential to be an anchor for a "creative and cultural production innovation district".

But this claim that has attracted scorn from figures in the art world.

The GSA's new report into restoration plans says several factors - including construction inflation, market volatility and economic pressure - have made the reinstatement of the Mackintosh far more expensive than predicted in a previous report, published in 2021.

The report states fully restoring the Mackintosh is therefore "dependent on external funding sources that are uncertain", and cites a "significant funding gap" needing to be resolved.

It believes the total cost of restoring the Mackintosh would be £265m.

Ann Priest, the chair of the school's board of governors, told BBC Scotland News: "What we are doing is what is affordable to us.

"The school cannot possibly, on its own, rebuild this whole building. It would take us away from financial sustainability.

"We are still hoping to go towards a full, faithful reinstatement. It's not going to be an exact replica, due to changes in construction and building [standards]."

She said the school would now look for "visionary individuals" who could bring "serious investment" to the project, as well as governments and local authorities.

The GSA will now remove external scaffolding about the building, saying this will improve the building's presentation and visibility, as well as helping stabilise the site.

Priest added that this will be the first step in "the reinstatement for whatever this building is going to become" and called the new proposals a landmark move for the long-term future of the site.

She said: "We have looked at the economic case, what does Glasgow need, what do our students need and what will benefit the school, the building and the creative and cultural industries."

The painter and writer Lachlan Goudie told BBC Scotland News the possibility of the art school not being restored to its former self was a disgrace.

He said he feared the more time passes from the fires, the higher chance people will start to forget the building.

Goudie said: "This reeks of a lack of transparency, which has been very common over the past eight years. The fact it has taken that long to get to a point where we know nothing much, beyond the GSA don't have enough money to rebuild it, is shameful.

"It is a national disgrace what has happened here. Nothing less than an authentic recreation of what Mackintosh left Glasgow and the world will do.

"We had the World Cup recently, and we should be as proud of the building as we are of the national football team. The Scottish government need to be saying this is vital to our national character - we had architectural crown jewels left to us."

When asked by BBC Scotland if she would consider the Mackintosh being taken outwith the board of governors and perhaps be passed to a trust if they fail to secure further investment, Priest said she was confident more funding would be achieved.

The first fire ripped through the Art School building on 23 May 2014.

Four years later an even more devastating fire broke out - this time occurring near the end of a £35m restoration project.

Since then GSA said about £26m of funding has gone towards work on restoring the historic building, which was first completed in 1910.

However work stalled after a dispute with insurers, resulting in a long-running arbitration claim. GSA now say this was settled to its satisfaction last month.

But it declined to reveal any details of what was awarded in the case, only saying the total came short of the number needed to carry out a full restoration of the building.

The report lists several cultural organisations based near Sauchiehall Street, including Scottish Opera, the Royal Conservatoire of Scotland and the National Theatre of Scotland.

It proposes formal recognition of this so-called cluster, saying it would be "a creative and culture-led innovation district would bring significant opportunities" for Glasgow as a whole, with the Mackintosh serving as a "cultural anchor" to the plan.

The Art School says it will seek to begin discussions immediately with the city, government, stakeholders and potential partners over this proposal.

However the mention of this cluster, which was not previously discussed in prior papers, has raised scepticism from some.

Prof Alan Dunlop, who studied architecture at the GSA, has previously criticised GSA management's handling of the two fires and ran for chair of the board in 2022.

He told BBC Scotland News the new plans feature "so many if's and but's".

He added: "This seems to me to be a dilution of responsibility, and worse, a distinct failure to focus on their prime task of delivering a replacement for the Mackintosh building, rather than enabling some centre for creative activity.

"There's no legitimate pathway described at all for the delivery for the 'faithful reinstatement' of the building."

Prof Dunlop says he fears the building is gone forever, as it would be impossible to rebuild to modern health and safety standards.

He also queried how the £265m figure had been reached by the GSA.

Priest told BBC Scotland the figure had been calculated through work with various leading architects.
Entertainment.description: Glasgow School of Art says it cannot fully restore the city's iconic Mackintosh Building to its former state without additional financial support. The art school previously insisted it was committed to a faithful reinstatement of the building, which was designed by Charles Rennie Mackintosh but wrecked by two fires...

Entertainment.title: YouTuber MrBeast marries Thea Booysen in British Virgin Islands
Entertainment.text:
YouTube star MrBeast has tied the knot with his fiancee Thea Booysen.

The pair married on Richard Branson's private Necker Island in the British Virgin Islands, according to US outlets.

The 28-year-old US content creator announced he had "found MrsBeast" in a joint Instagram post, external, which featured images of them posing in white on their big day.

MrBeast, real name Jimmy Donaldson, is the biggest YouTuber in the world with more than 500m subscribers.

Donaldson popped the question to Booysen on Christmas Day 2024 while they wore matching MrBeast-branded festive jumpers.

He previously told US outlet People magazine that despite being known for huge online stunts, he wanted a "really private and intimate" proposal and small wedding.

In photos shared by the couple, Booysen can be seen wearing a floor length lace wedding dress with a floral pattern, while Donaldson opted for a white tuxedo.

Booysen also had several outfit changes throughout the day, including a white mini dress with a sparkly bodice and feathered skirt for cutting the cake, and an off-the-shoulder satin look for later in the evening.

MrBeast described it as "the best day of my life" in the post.

The pair married on Richard Branson's private Necker Island in the British Virgin Islands, according to US outlets. The 28-year-old US content creator announced he had "found MrsBeast" in a joint Instagram post, external, which featured images of...

Entertainment.title: City celebrates Ozzy Day on anniversary of Ozzy Osbourne's death
Entertainment.text:
Birmingham is marking a year since the death of Ozzy Osbourne with a day of music and celebration, affectionately called "Ozzy Day", to commemorate the passing of one of its most beloved sons.

The city has been awash with fans cloaked in black - wearing T-shirts in tribute to Osbourne or Black Sabbath - visiting sites in the city most connected to the heavy metal icon.

Fans queued at New Street station to have their photo taken with the mechanical Ozzy the bull statue, which has been specially dressed up for Ozzy Day, receiving a free commemorative Polaroid.

Some even travelled internationally to celebrate the star in the city that made him.

Among the crowds queuing for a photo was Julie Sawyer and her daughter Holly, who travelled from Nottingham to pay their respects.

Julie lost her husband, Holly's father, in March 2025.

Paul Sawyer was a big fan of Osbourne and had Crazy Train played at his funeral.

Julie said it was emotional to be in the city for Ozzy Day, but they "couldn't not be here".

In an interview with the BBC, Sharon Osbourne said she wanted fans to remember her husband by playing his music "really loud".

Black Sabbath bandmate Tony Iommi has also paid tribute on social media, saying: "Well, it's now a year since Ozzy's passing and I still can't believe that he's not with us.

"I watch him on YouTube and it feels like he's still here with us. We always stayed in touch, almost every week or so and we'd usually talk about our ailments and then about what we'd been doing that particular week.

"The fans have been absolutely brilliant and the love and respect that they have shown for him has just been amazing.

"I'm so glad that we were able to play one final show with the original line up of Sabbath at the Back To The Beginning show in our home town of Birmingham!

"There will never be another Ozzy. I miss you my dear friend."

The city has been awash with fans cloaked in black - wearing T-shirts in tribute to Osbourne or Black Sabbath - visiting...

Entertainment.title: How a former Blue Peter presenter stunned America's Got Talent judges
Entertainment.text:
A 50th birthday gift from her fellow Spice Girls, a picture of a dog and a mobile phone.

Those were the prized possessions that three America's Got Talent judges watched a former Blue Peter presenter appear to smash with a hammer.

But like all magic tricks, everything was not quite as it seemed and moments later the treasured items returned.

Joel Mawhinney, known as Joel M, said the "risky" trick was "the scariest experience" of his life.

But as he reminded the judges: "Everything that disappears must reappear."

Going into the judges dressing rooms and taking some of their prized possessions might not be the best way of getting them on your side.

Smashing those items with a hammer is even less likely to help your chances.

Yet that is exactly what Mawhinney did, leaving Mel B, Sofía Vergara and Howie Mandel stunned before revealing that every item had somehow survived.

Mandel's phone appeared inside a locked box that had been sitting in front of the judges throughout the audition.

The phone then began receiving texts from Mawhinney revealing where the other judges missing valuables had ended up.

Vergara's framed picture of her dog was hidden inside her seat cushion, while Mel B's bracelet was concealed in one of the famous Got Talent red buzzers.

"I don't know how you do it. I believe in magic but I loved it," Mel B said when giving her verdict, which was a yes.

Vergara laughed and said the "magic was very impressive". She also voted yes, adding: "I don't know if this was necessary, but it was very exciting."

Mendel called Mawhinney "charming, you've got a good look", but it was a no from the comedian, calling the performance "too slow".

Simon Cowell agreed on the length of the performance and said "you can't do that on a live show".

"Our audience love magic, which is exactly the reason I'm going to say yes."

With three yeses, Mawhinney progressed to the next stage and remains in with a chance of winning the cash prize.

Mawhinney said that when he entered the judges' dressing rooms he didn't know what he would have ended up with.

"I knew the plan was to take prized possessions, or whatever I could find, but it just so happened that the jewellery was there," he told BBC Good Morning Ulster programme.

"It's a hard and set rule that if I'm performing magic, I don't borrow possessions of people.

"I don't like taking phones. I don't like borrowing rings because I've just heard so many horror stories.

"But I thought, it's America's Got Talent, I have to do something. So I sort of went for it."

The former presenter said producers had been inviting him to audition for America's Got Talent since 2020, but he always felt it sounded like "a massive risk" and his "gut instinct was always no".

"But then I left Blue Peter this year and wanted to establish myself as a magician again, because that's what I'm doing now.

"And I thought, you might as well kick the whole thing off with something a bit extreme, so I'll fly to the States and do that."

Those were the prized possessions that three America's Got Talent judges watched a former Blue Peter presenter appear to smash with a hammer. But like all magic tricks, everything was not quite as it seemed and moments later...

Entertainment.title: BBC Introducing stage axed from Reading Festival
Entertainment.text:
A stage at Reading Festival which previously hosted up-and-coming musicians including Ed Sheeran and Florence + the Machine has been axed.

Organisers Festival Republic announced an overhaul of stages last week, saying it would now be open to a "much wider pool of emerging talent".

As part of the plans, it said it would no longer host a BBC Introducing stage, despite the scheme having supported roughly 70% of all the UK artists on this year's line-up.

Tommy Longhurst, lead singer of Reading band Only the Poets, said the move was "sad news" for young musicians.

He said the music industry is already "really hard" for those who are just starting out, with grassroots venues struggling to stay open.

He said he believed the BBC Introducing stage "really does a lot for new artists".

Only the Poets have played several sets on the BBC Introducing stage at their home festival in previous years, most recently in 2025.

Longhurst said: "We hold those memories so highly and it was such a poignant moment for us to get to play a festival that inspired us to start the band.

"For me I think we're taking a huge opportunity from up and coming artists."

Organisers Festival Republic said its new Canopy stage would become the home for new and breaking acts.

It added: "Previously, only artists who had been selected by the BBC were qualified to play the Introducing Stage - now, we are able to open up this important platform to a much wider pool of emerging talent and book any new music artist for the stage.

"The change is fundamentally about supporting a more diverse range of up-and-coming artists, who will hopefully go on to headline the festival in years to come."
Entertainment.description: A stage at Reading Festival which previously hosted up-and-coming musicians including Ed Sheeran and Florence + the Machine has been axed. Organisers Festival Republic announced an overhaul of stages last week, saying it would now be open to a "much wider pool of emerging talent". As part of the plans, it said it...

Entertainment.title: Judge orders pause on Paramount-Warner merger after challenge from 12 states
Entertainment.text:
A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court.

Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week – alleging that such a combination would “extinguish competition” in Hollywood and lead to fewer choices for consumers, particularly moviegoers and cable customers across the US.

The states’ top prosecutors called on Warner and Paramount to not close the transaction until after a court had time to “fully evaluate” their claims. And when the companies refused, they filed for a temporary restraining order – which is what district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín granted on Monday. That opens the door to a potential preliminary injunction that the states are also seeking to effectively block the deal.

“This is a critical first win in our case to ensure this megamerger never sees the light of day,” Rob Bonta, the California attorney general, said in a statement following Monday’s order. “History tells the tale of what happens when a few people have great power over markets that are central to Americans’ lives: fewer opportunities for more people, worse products and services for all people.”

A Warner-Paramount tie-up would bring together two of the five last legacy studios in Hollywood – as well as host of TV networks, titles filling streaming libraries and news operations. That would include Warner’s HBO Max, fan favorites such as Harry Potter and even CNN coming under the same roof of Paramount-owned CBS, movies including Top Gun and the Paramount+ streaming service.

Paramount did not immediately comment on Monday’s order. But the company, which was bought out by Skydance just last year, has vowed to “vigorously defend” its Warner acquisition. Paramount previously called the states’ complaint “wrong on both the facts and the law”, maintaining that a merger would instead strengthen competition against bigger entertainment rivals. And it touted regulatory greenlights the deal has received elsewhere, including from the Trump administration last month.

The temporary restraining order granted on Monday halts the deal from progressing for at least 14 days, although the pause could be extended for up to 28 days. The court has set 3 August as a date for a hearing on the states’ preliminary injunction motion, although that schedule could also be pushed back.
Entertainment.description: A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount and Warner Bros Discovery to halt their $81bn merger for at least two weeks, allowing states that are challenging the deal more time to see their case through in court. Twelve states, led by California, sued to block Paramount’s pending buyout of Warner last week –...

BreakingNewsTab.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will reportedly allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.

The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program", according to the US energy department.

Text from the deal was not made public, but US media have reported that it could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also to make nuclear bombs.

Experts say that would mark a first for the US and could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.

The US Department of Energy noted that along with the co-operation deal, a "bilateral safeguards agreement" was also signed.

"Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," the statement reads.

The Saudi government also released a statement, saying the deal "represents an extension of existing co-operation between the two countries in the energy sector", and noted that it comes after the Saudi leader visited Washington last November.

The announcement comes as fighting escalates between the US and Iran. Saudi Arabia, a longtime US ally, hosts several US bases, which have been targeted by Iranian attacks. It also comes two days after Iran's allies in Yemen, the Houthis, announced a "maritime blockade" against the kingdom.

Specific details of the nuclear deal were not given but earlier media reports , externalsuggested that, after undertaking a joint study with the US, Saudi Arabia may be allowed to enrich uranium on its soil to produce fuel at a US-built enrichment facility, instead of relying on imported fuel.

This could be worth billions of dollars to the US, whose companies could be involved in building a facility to produce enriched uranium, which can be used as fuel for power plants but also to make nuclear bombs.

"Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world's best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States," US Secretary of Energy Chris Wright said in a statement.

The energy department said the deal advances both "US and regional security" and will uphold "high standards of nuclear safety, security, and nonproliferation and strengthening the United States' competitive edge in civil nuclear technology".

Nuclear experts and former officials in Israel - the US's top ally in the Middle East - voiced concern the plan could ultimately lead to Saudi Arabia being able to develop a nuclear weapon - though many experts say that path would likely take decades and lead to pushback from the international community.

Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the deal includes the US allowing Saudi Arabia to have facilities to enrich uranium.

"The US has never done that before, we have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding the reason for that is because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.

The agreement will now be sent to Congress for review.

Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party currently controls both the upper and lower chambers.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio pushed back against critics who may be concerned about Saudi Arabia's ability to develop a nuclear weapon in the future.

"The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation," Rubio told reporters while travelling in the Philippines.

Several Democrats who voiced opposition to the deal noted that during his time as a senator, Rubio had endorsed a law - the "No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act" - which required Congress to sign off on a nuclear deal with the Saudis.

Massachusetts Democratic Senator Edward Markey issued a statement condemning Rubio and Trump, and saying that their "hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness".

"They are allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb," said Markey.

If an enrichment provision is included, it would distinguish this agreement from another one that was struck by the US in 2009 with Saudi Arabia's neighbour, the United Arab Emirates, under which the UAE does not produce its own fuel and accepts a host of other restrictions.

For that reason, the agreement is also expected to attract the concern of nuclear non-proliferation campaigners, whose ultimate aim is to prevent an increase in the number of nuclear weapons worldwide.

Saudi Arabia has been lobbying for such a deal for years under multiple presidents, but Washington has held out with a key request that the country recognise Israel, a key US ally, and normalise diplomatic and economic relations with it.

But that demand appears to have been dropped as a condition for the deal, according to US media reports.

Trump has worked to cultivate a closer relationship with Saudi Arabia during his time in the White House.

Along with the strategic and commercial gains likely to come with this deal, one possible reason for this agreement happening now is Russia and China have reportedly been in discussions on something similar and the US might not have wanted to miss out.

The deal marks a major victory for Saudi Arabia, and it has the potential to reshape power dynamics in the Middle East - though not immediately and perhaps not for several years, if not longer.

The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran. The two nations say one of the reasons they attacked Iran in February was to prevent the country from ever developing a nuclear weapon - something that Tehran denies planning to do.

Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".
BreakingNewsTab.description: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will reportedly allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme. The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program", according to the...
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Houthis claim attack on Saudi tankers in Red Sea
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Yemen's Houthis say they have attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea after the US launched its 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran.

Iranian state media said two people had been killed in the US strikes on Wednesday, and a tanker caught fire in an explosion in a mined route in the Strait of Hormuz.

Ahead of the latest round of strikes, US President Donald Trump had threatened to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant if Iran attacked a ship in the strait and to "take care of" the Houthis if they blocked the Red Sea.

If confirmed, the Houthi attack would be the first since the Iran-backed group announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia.

The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though neither their responsibility, nor the attack on the Layla has been confirmed.

The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade.

Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation".

UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.

British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit, according to news agency Reuters.

US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".

Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.

Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq border.

Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Reuters reported.

The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.

One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the Strait, according to Iranian state media.

It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.

On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said.
BreakingNewsTab.description: Yemen's Houthis say they have attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea after the US launched its 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Iranian state media said two people had been killed in the US strikes on Wednesday, and a tanker caught fire in an explosion in a mined route in the Strait of Hormuz....
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: PC Harper's widow criticises early prisoner release plan as Burnham to review scheme
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The widow of PC Andrew Harper has described the possible early release of prisoners as "deplorable", following reports that two of his killers could be freed early.

Lissie Harper said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims.

Prime Minister Andy Burnham earlier said he would review the Sentencing Act policy that from September would see thousands of offenders in England and Wales eligible to be released earlier.

Speaking on Wednesday, Burnham said: "I can't say that I can change the policy completely, but I will look at it in detail before we go further forward."

He acknowledged that there was "pressure on prisons places" and that a failure to invest in the prison system in the past had created "an unacceptable situation".

Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers were sentenced to 13 years in custody for manslaughter but could be released early under the new Sentencing Act.

PC Harper was killed after he was dragged to his death behind their getaway car. The car's driver, Henry Long, was jailed for 16 years for manslaughter but would not be eligible for early release.

Burnham's comments came after the mother of police officer Andrew Harper said the potential early release of two of her son's killers felt like an "insult".

In a separate statement released later on Wednesday, PC Harper's widow Lissie Harper said: "It has taken me years to rebuild a life that was torn apart in a matter of moments. Years to pick up the pieces left behind by three men whose actions robbed Andrew of his life and stole our future.

"It is deplorable to me that we have reached a point where releasing prisoners early is even considered acceptable. We speak of justice, accountability and standing with victims, yet decisions like this do the opposite."

The overcrowding of the prison estate has been a problem for several years and successive governments have released prisoners early in a bid to free up space.

Tens of thousands of inmates have been let out of prison to create more space in jails since 2024.

The Sentencing Act, which was passed by Sir Keir Starmer's government earlier this year, was designed to ease pressure on prisons, and will see some offenders spend less time in prison before release.

Burnham said: "I want to assure myself that everything has been looked at before this policy proceeds, and I can give that assurance that every single part of this I will look into with the justice secretary."

Concerning the case of PC Harper, he said: "I feel for the family it's an appalling crime that was committed."

He said the "main perpetrator" of his death would not be eligible for early release.

Burnham also said it was not acceptable to joke about early releases and that it was right Defence Secretary Wes Streeting had apologised for comments he made that were caught on camera.

The prison population in England and Wales has risen steeply since the mid-1990s, doubling in size between 1992 and 2012.

Shortly after coming into power, the Labour government, faced with warnings that prisons would run out of space within days, released thousands of inmates.

The Sentencing Act reduces the point of release meaning some prisoners could be let out after serving a third of their sentence, while those who would previously have served two-thirds will instead be released after serving half of their sentence.

The legislation also says courts should stop handing out short prison sentences of under a year, other than in specific or exceptional circumstances.

Katie Kempen, chief executive at Victim Support, said: "It's encouraging to hear the new PM will be looking again at the early release policy.

"This review must urgently fix the fact that most victims currently have no way of knowing whether or not an offender could be released early."

Conservative shadow justice minister Nick Timothy said: "Labour's early release scheme has been catastrophic. Victims should not have to live in fear all summer waiting for a review.

"It would be shameful if the killers of PC Andrew Harper, grooming gang perpetrators and rapists were to get out of prison early, but this is what Labour had planned."

Thames Valley Police Chief Constable Jason Hogg said he had written to the government to make it clear he believed the early release of Jessie Cole and Albert Bowers would be wrong.

There are just under 3,000 vacant spaces in prisons across England and Wales, more than this time two years ago when they were on the verge of reaching full capacity.

The Sentencing Act was created to help alleviate some of that pressure by letting out more than 5,000 inmates earlier than their potential release date so that more spaces would be created and staff weren't overstretched.

If Burnham were to pull the plans, prison staff have told the BBC it could be "highly problematic".

They had been expecting thousands to be released in the autumn under the new law - freeing up cells for people handed custodial sentences.

One senior prison staffer said "with such little headroom, we need more cells to put people in. Otherwise it's going to get hairy again. We don't want a repeat of 2024."
BreakingNewsTab.description: The widow of PC Andrew Harper has described the possible early release of prisoners as "deplorable", following reports that two of his killers could be freed early. Lissie Harper said politicians were "making decisions from behind a desk that will have real and lasting consequences" for the families of victims....
BreakingNewsTab.author: SkunkNet Wire - bbc.co.uk

BreakingNewsTab.title: Most bus fares in England to be capped at £2 from January
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced.

The government says the policy, which is expected to cost more than £500m, will "help with the cost of living and give people the breathing space they need".

At the start of last year, the England-wide bus fare cap rose to £3 under Sir Keir Starmer's Labour government, although in Liverpool and Manchester fares were held at £2.

Burnham said affordable transport links were an essential, adding: "Lower fares will help people get to where they need to."

The measure gives people "a little bit of hope" in a time when many are grappling with higher living costs - especially after fuel costs spiked as a result of the US-Iran war, transport secretary Heidi Alexander told the BBC's Today programme.

However, she admitted the measure was "not the whole solution".

The new cap will take effect from January next year for fares on participating buses outside of London. The cost of a non-discounted single bus journey in London is £1.75.

The Burnham government said going back to a £2 cap should particularly help passengers in rural and coastal areas, where single fares can be higher.

The Campaign for Better Transport said rural areas were still suffering from fewer bus routes and services, adding that such areas had been more affected by council cut backs in the last decade than urban zones.

Services in those areas - as measured by vehicle miles - decreased by 18% between 2019 and 2024, according to the County Councils Network, which represents 39 unitary and county councils across England.

"What we need to see is the replacing of those lost services which have particularly affected those in rural areas," Campaign for Better Transport's Michael Solomon Williams said.

When first introduced in 2023, the £2 cap was initially meant both as a cost of living measure and to encourage people back onto buses in the wake of the pandemic.

It played a role in reducing inflation that year, the Office for National Statistics said.

An evaluation of the first ten months found that the scheme contributed 5% of the 13% rise in bus usage during that period. Lower-income passengers felt the most financial benefit.

However it did not score highly when it came to value for money. How much money people saved varied depending how much fares in their local area cost before the cap.

In those first ten months, £210m was spent on the scheme, less than the £245m budget.

Even if fare-capping measures had increased demand for bus travel, numbers are still recovering from the huge drop in usage during Covid.

By March 2025, 1.9 billion journeys were made on local buses in England outside of London - around 93% of pre-pandemic levels.
BreakingNewsTab.description: The bus fare cap in England will revert to £2 from January, new prime minister Andy Burnham has announced. The government says the policy, which is expected to cost more than £500m, will "help with the cost of living and give people the breathing space they need". At the start of last year, the England-wide bus...

BreakingNewsTab.title: Ukrainian drones hit more sites of Russian online giant retailer Wildberries
BreakingNewsTab.text:
Ukrainian drones have hit warehouses of Russia's biggest online retailer again, killing one person and injuring another 14, local officials say.

They say logistics hubs run by Wildberries in two south-west cities in the Krasnodar and Stavropol regions were struck overnight, triggering the evacuation of employees.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said the hubs were "involved in providing the Russian army with drone components, navigation equipment and other gear".

Ukraine hit the retailer's warehouses in central Russia four days ago, killing eight people, according to Russian officials.

Wildberries - often described as Russia's Amazon - employs some 48,000 people.

Workers were evacuated after the attacks on logistics centres in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk, Wildberries founder and Russia's wealthiest woman Tatyana Kim said earlier on Wednesday.

"There are no words to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on us, who are simply doing our jobs," she wrote on Telegram. "But these situations demonstrate the most important thing: we must continue to do what we do for the benefit of people."

In Krasnodar, 10 people were injured in an attack on a warehouse complex, with three in a serious condition, the region's Governor Veniamin Kondratyev said.

He later said that a young woman died of her injuries in hospital.

Five people were hurt in a warehouse complex on the outskirts of Nevinnomyssk, Stavropol Governor Vladimir Vladimirov said.

BBC Verify has analysed footage and confirmed fires in the two locations.

Several hours after the latest attacks, a range of military gear - including body armour and drone components - was widely available on Wildberries' website, a quick search by the BBC showed.

Serhii Kuzan, chairman of the Ukrainian Security and Cooperation Centre, has described Wildberries as a "vital component" of Russian logistics, adding that the firm's website is being used by Russian volunteers to buy military equipment.

"The primary rationale for striking Wildberries' warehouses is to disrupt Russian logistics and the supply of dual-use goods, critical electronics, sanctioned goods and the like to the Russian army and Russian arms manufacturers," Kuzan told the BBC.

"The collateral damage from such strikes could also have a serious impact on the Russian economy, as well as a psychological effect on Russian society and, likely, on continued support for the war."

The Ukrainian strikes have also dealt a serious blow to Russia's small and mid-sized businesses, angering hundreds of sellers whose livelihoods depend on online marketplaces.

They say logistics hubs run by Wildberries in two south-west cities in the Krasnodar and Stavropol regions were struck overnight, triggering the evacuation of employees....

BreakingNewsTab.title: Multiple sclerosis: First drug to help patients walk now on NHS in England
BreakingNewsTab.text:
The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England.

Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently.

Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a "huge difference".

Up to 5,000 people in England could benefit each year. The drug is already available elsewhere in the UK.

Multiple sclerosis is caused by the immune system attacking the protective covering around nerve cells - known as myelin.

This damage impairs the ability of nerves to carry electrical messages from the brain to the rest of the body - including those needed for the precise control of muscles needed to walk.

Fampridine is known as a "signal booster" as it helps those electrical messages still get through.

Clinical trials showed around four in 10 people taking the drug had improvements in their walking speed and in how long they could walk for.

This could be the difference between getting around the house or needing help at home or enabling people to go out and see friends and family.

Current NHS support includes physiotherapy and walking aids.

The drug has previously been rejected for use by the NHS in England as it was not considered value for money, although it did become available in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland.

Aysen Slack, 65 and from Eastbourne, chose to pay privately for the pills once she heard about them.

She said fampridine was "working well for me" but came at a "significant expense and I could not keep doing that forever" so she had to stop.

Slack said it was "great news" the drug was coming to the NHS and she "would definitely like to try" again.

"My mobility has decreased a lot and even in my flat I have to use sticks now," she said, "it would make a huge difference to my life if I were able to improve my walking."

More than 120,000 people live with MS in England. However, only around 5,000 people a year will be eligible - based on the extent of their walking difficulties.

Patients will try the medicine for up to a month and only those showing "clear benefit" will be able to continue taking it.

Prof Frankie Swords, the NHS national medical director, said: "Walking difficulties can have a huge impact on the freedom and independence of people with MS, so this signal-boosting pill could be life-changing for thousands of patients.

"Even a modest improvement could mean getting around the house more easily, going out with greater confidence and living more independently."

Ceri Smith, from the MS Society, said they were "delighted" by the news as the drug could be "life-changing" for many, allowing people to "live more independently or stay in employment".
BreakingNewsTab.description: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is being made available on the NHS in England. Fampridine boosts nerve signals to make it easier for some people to move and live independently. Aysen Slack, who used to pay for fampridine privately until it became too costly, said the decision would make a...

BreakingNewsTab.title: UK weather: When will it rain next?
BreakingNewsTab.text:
If you cannot remember the last time it rained, you are probably not alone.

June was actually wetter than average in the UK, which might come as a surprise given our recent run of weather, but for England and Wales the majority of that rain came down in the first two weeks of the month.

Since 15 June, most of England has recorded less than 10mm of rain with large areas of central southern England seeing 3mm or less. Meanwhile, Wisley in Surrey has had no rain at all for 36 consecutive days.

Most of Wales has seen less than 25mm (1in) of rain since the middle of June, but it has been particularly dry in the north-east with Rhyl in Denbighshire recording only 3mm.

A Thames Water hosepipe ban came into force on Thursday, adding to other bans already in place for customers of other water companies across the south of the UK, as parts of southern, central and eastern England were declared to be close to drought.

Northern Ireland had a very wet June, recording 67% more rain than average, and it was also very wet in western Scotland. However, July has been much drier than average in Northern Ireland and most of Scotland, though the rain has continued in the Highlands, with Aultbea already reaching the July average rainfall, and with more on the way.

This weekend will bring a change in our weather patterns.

Normally weather systems move eastwards with time. However, the high pressure we have had for a long time now is actually going to move westward, in something known as retrogression.

This will allow the jet stream to move right over the UK and steer weather fronts in, bringing some welcome rain.

The hills of Northern Ireland, northern England and north Wales will have 5-10mm of rain this weekend.

Western Scotland will be wetter, with 10-20mm over high ground.

The rain is likely to be lighter and patchier in the Midlands, southern and east England with just a few millimetres expected and some areas could miss out altogether.

June was actually wetter than average in the UK, which might come as a surprise given our recent run of weather, but for England and Wales the majority of that rain came down in the first two weeks of the month. Since 15 June, most of...
